retrievedChunks.title: 1440p gaming allocation
retrievedChunks.content: 1440p gaming usually benefits from a GPU-first budget with a balanced modern CPU. It is the broadest value target for high-refresh PC gaming.

retrievedChunks.title: RTX 4070 Super gaming profile
retrievedChunks.content: The RTX 4070 Super is efficient for high-quality 1440p gaming and supports CUDA, but its 12GB VRAM is less flexible for larger local AI workloads.

retrievedChunks.title: RX 7800 XT 1440p value
retrievedChunks.content: The RX 7800 XT is a strong value target for 1440p gaming with 16GB VRAM. It favors raster performance per dollar over CUDA ecosystem support.

retrievedChunks.title: RTX 4070 Ti Super balanced profile
retrievedChunks.content: The RTX 4070 Ti Super provides 16GB VRAM and strong 1440p to 4K capability at lower power and cost than flagship GPUs. It is a balanced choice for mixed gaming, creative, and CUDA work.

retrievedChunks.title: 1080p gaming allocation
retrievedChunks.content: At 1080p and high refresh rates, CPU frame delivery matters more than at higher resolutions. GPU budget remains important, but extreme GPU spending can produce diminishing returns.

retrievedChunks.title: RTX 5070 Ti balanced high-end profile
retrievedChunks.content: The GeForce RTX 5070 Ti provides 16GB GDDR7 and CUDA at a 300W reference power target. It is positioned for high-refresh 1440p, entry 4K, creator acceleration, and moderate local AI workloads without flagship cost.

retrievedChunks.title: Intel Arc B580 value profile
retrievedChunks.content: Intel Arc B580 Limited Edition provides 12GB VRAM, 190W total board power, AV1 media support, and a $249 recommended customer price. It is relevant to value 1080p/1440p builds, while application and game-specific driver behavior should be verified for specialized workloads.
retrievedChunks.sourceTitle: Intel Arc B580 product specifications and ordering

retrievedChunks.title: Core i7-14700K high-refresh gaming profile
retrievedChunks.content: The Core i7-14700K is an Intel LGA1700 option for high-refresh gaming and mixed productivity. Its high peak power requires substantial cooling, and the mature socket has less future CPU upgrade room than newer platforms.

retrievedChunks.title: Ryzen 7 9800X3D gaming focus
retrievedChunks.content: The Ryzen 7 9800X3D is an enthusiast gaming CPU. Its value is strongest in high-refresh gaming systems where the GPU is already well funded.

retrievedChunks.title: Radeon RX 9070 XT RDNA 4 profile
retrievedChunks.content: The Radeon RX 9070 XT has 16GB GDDR6, a 304W typical board power rating, and a 750W minimum PSU recommendation from AMD. It is a strong raster and FSR-oriented 4K gaming option, but users requiring CUDA-first software should still prefer NVIDIA.

retrievedChunks.title: Case clearance constraints
retrievedChunks.content: GPU length, air-cooler height, motherboard form factor, and PSU form factor are hard physical constraints. These values must be checked deterministically before a case is accepted.

retrievedChunks.title: Ryzen 7 7800X3D gaming profile
retrievedChunks.content: The Ryzen 7 7800X3D remains a focused gaming option with lower platform power than many high-core-count alternatives. It is less compelling for heavily threaded workstation tasks.

retrievedChunks.title: RX 7900 XTX raster value
retrievedChunks.content: The RX 7900 XTX offers 24GB VRAM and excellent high-resolution raster performance. It is compelling for 4K gaming when CUDA compatibility is not required.

retrievedChunks.title: Peerless Assassin value cooling
retrievedChunks.content: The Peerless Assassin 120 SE offers high air-cooling capacity at low cost. It is well suited to mainstream and many high-tier CPUs when case height allows it.

retrievedChunks.title: Ryzen 9 9950X3D mixed gaming and creation profile
retrievedChunks.content: Ryzen 9 9950X3D combines 16 Zen 5 cores with second-generation 3D V-Cache on AM5. It fits premium systems that need strong gaming latency and high multicore throughput, but it should not displace cheaper eight-core X3D options in gaming-only budgets.

retrievedChunks.title: 4K gaming allocation
retrievedChunks.content: At 4K the GPU is normally the primary performance constraint. Budget should favor the graphics card while retaining enough CPU, cooling, and PSU quality to avoid platform imbalance.

retrievedChunks.title: PCIe 5.0 SSD performance and thermal trade-off
retrievedChunks.content: High-end PCIe 5.0 SSDs such as Samsung 9100 Pro can exceed 14GB/s sequential reads, but cost and heat are materially higher than mature PCIe 4.0 drives. Gaming load times rarely justify allocating budget away from the GPU solely for peak sequential throughput; large creator and data workflows benefit more.

retrievedChunks.title: 4K 240Hz target trade-off
retrievedChunks.content: A 4K 240Hz target is exceptionally demanding and depends heavily on the game, settings, and upscaling. The recommendation should maximize GPU headroom without claiming that every title will sustain 240 FPS.

result.explanation:
## Summary
This $1,162 build targets 1440p 144Hz gaming with a black, non-RGB traditional case. All components are compatible, and the power supply provides adequate headroom.

## Trade-offs
The GPU choice prioritizes raster performance and VRAM over CUDA support [K3], which may limit AI workloads but aligns with the gaming focus. The CPU selection balances cost with 1440p gaming demands [K1], avoiding overspending on frame delivery that benefits lower resolutions more [K5].

## Upgrade path
Adding a higher-tier GPU like the RTX 4070 Ti Super would improve 1440p performance and enable CUDA workloads [K4], but requires a larger budget. The current platform supports future CPU upgrades without changing the motherboard or RAM.

retrievedChunks.title: Power supply headroom rule
retrievedChunks.content: A power supply should exceed estimated sustained system draw by at least 35 percent. Additional headroom can reduce fan noise and preserve room for future GPU upgrades.

retrievedChunks.title: 4K gaming allocation
retrievedChunks.content: At 4K the GPU is normally the primary performance constraint. Budget should favor the graphics card while retaining enough CPU, cooling, and PSU quality to avoid platform imbalance.

retrievedChunks.title: 4K 240Hz target trade-off
retrievedChunks.content: A 4K 240Hz target is exceptionally demanding and depends heavily on the game, settings, and upscaling. The recommendation should maximize GPU headroom without claiming that every title will sustain 240 FPS.

retrievedChunks.title: Fractal North balanced airflow
retrievedChunks.content: The Fractal North supports full-size ATX components with 355mm GPU clearance and 170mm air-cooler clearance. It balances airflow, acoustics, and upgrade room.

retrievedChunks.title: RTX 4070 Ti Super balanced profile
retrievedChunks.content: The RTX 4070 Ti Super provides 16GB VRAM and strong 1440p to 4K capability at lower power and cost than flagship GPUs. It is a balanced choice for mixed gaming, creative, and CUDA work.

retrievedChunks.title: 850W Gold mainstream high-end fit
retrievedChunks.content: An 850W Gold PSU is a common fit for high-end single-GPU systems when it satisfies the 1.35x power rule. It offers more upgrade margin than 750W without flagship PSU cost.

retrievedChunks.title: RTX 5070 Ti balanced high-end profile
retrievedChunks.content: The GeForce RTX 5070 Ti provides 16GB GDDR7 and CUDA at a 300W reference power target. It is positioned for high-refresh 1440p, entry 4K, creator acceleration, and moderate local AI workloads without flagship cost.

retrievedChunks.title: RTX 5080 4K and creator profile
retrievedChunks.content: The GeForce RTX 5080 reference design combines 16GB GDDR7, CUDA support, a 360W total graphics power rating, and Blackwell media/AI features. It is a high-end 4K and creator option below the RTX 5090, but 16GB VRAM is a meaningful limit for larger local AI models.

retrievedChunks.title: RTX 5090 flagship profile
retrievedChunks.content: The RTX 5090 combines 32GB GDDR7 with CUDA support and a 575W total graphics power rating. It requires an enthusiast budget, a compatible case, and at least a 1000W system power plan.

retrievedChunks.title: Peerless Assassin value cooling
retrievedChunks.content: The Peerless Assassin 120 SE offers high air-cooling capacity at low cost. It is well suited to mainstream and many high-tier CPUs when case height allows it.

retrievedChunks.title: 1080p gaming allocation
retrievedChunks.content: At 1080p and high refresh rates, CPU frame delivery matters more than at higher resolutions. GPU budget remains important, but extreme GPU spending can produce diminishing returns.

retrievedChunks.title: Ryzen 5 7600 value profile
retrievedChunks.content: The Ryzen 5 7600 preserves AM5 upgradeability while leaving more of a constrained gaming budget for the GPU.

retrievedChunks.title: X670E workstation expansion
retrievedChunks.content: X670E boards add expansion and connectivity for high-tier AM5 workstations. They should be selected for actual I/O or upgrade needs rather than prestige alone.

retrievedChunks.title: PCIe 5.0 SSD performance and thermal trade-off
retrievedChunks.content: High-end PCIe 5.0 SSDs such as Samsung 9100 Pro can exceed 14GB/s sequential reads, but cost and heat are materially higher than mature PCIe 4.0 drives. Gaming load times rarely justify allocating budget away from the GPU solely for peak sequential throughput; large creator and data workflows benefit more.

retrievedChunks.content: The Ryzen 7 7800X3D remains a focused gaming option with lower platform power than many high-core-count alternatives. It is less compelling for heavily threaded workstation tasks.

retrievedChunks.title: Ryzen 9 9950X3D mixed gaming and creation profile
retrievedChunks.content: Ryzen 9 9950X3D combines 16 Zen 5 cores with second-generation 3D V-Cache on AM5. It fits premium systems that need strong gaming latency and high multicore throughput, but it should not displace cheaper eight-core X3D options in gaming-only budgets.

retrievedChunks.title: AM5 platform upgradeability
retrievedChunks.content: AM5 boards provide a clearer CPU upgrade path than mature end-of-cycle platforms. The value is highest when the user explicitly plans a future CPU upgrade.

retrievedChunks.title: AM5 B850 and X870 platform choice
retrievedChunks.content: B850 boards can provide PCIe 5.0 storage and mainstream connectivity, while X870 boards add mandatory USB4-class connectivity and often more high-speed I/O. Board selection should follow actual M.2, USB4, LAN, Wi-Fi, and expansion needs rather than chipset prestige.

result.sourceQuery: Budget USD 5,000 for a local AI workstation: CUDA is required, target 70B Q4 inference plus FLUX image generation, at least 32 GB VRAM and 128 GB system RAM. Linux, quiet operation, no aesthetic priority.

retrievedChunks.title: VRAM is the primary local AI constraint
retrievedChunks.content: Local LLM inference and diffusion workloads keep model weights and intermediate data in GPU memory. 12GB suits smaller experiments, 16GB is a strong hobbyist target, and 24GB materially expands the models and batch sizes that fit without CPU offload.

retrievedChunks.title: RTX 4090 local AI profile
retrievedChunks.content: The RTX 4090 combines 24GB VRAM with CUDA support. It is a high-cost option best considered when local AI capacity matters more than power draw or compact size.

retrievedChunks.title: RTX 5080 4K and creator profile
retrievedChunks.content: The GeForce RTX 5080 reference design combines 16GB GDDR7, CUDA support, a 360W total graphics power rating, and Blackwell media/AI features. It is a high-end 4K and creator option below the RTX 5090, but 16GB VRAM is a meaningful limit for larger local AI models.

retrievedChunks.title: RTX 5060 Ti 16GB capacity-value trade-off
retrievedChunks.content: The 16GB RTX 5060 Ti offers more model and texture capacity than 8GB cards while retaining CUDA support and a 180W reference power target. Its compute throughput is midrange, so capacity-sensitive hobby AI can fit even when raw generation or training speed is below higher-tier GPUs.

retrievedChunks.title: RTX 5070 Ti balanced high-end profile
retrievedChunks.content: The GeForce RTX 5070 Ti provides 16GB GDDR7 and CUDA at a 300W reference power target. It is positioned for high-refresh 1440p, entry 4K, creator acceleration, and moderate local AI workloads without flagship cost.

retrievedChunks.title: CUDA ecosystem preference
retrievedChunks.content: Many common AI tools are optimized first for NVIDIA CUDA. AMD cards can offer strong raster value, but users who need broad framework compatibility should prioritize CUDA unless they have verified their exact stack.

retrievedChunks.title: RTX 4070 Ti Super balanced profile
retrievedChunks.content: The RTX 4070 Ti Super provides 16GB VRAM and strong 1440p to 4K capability at lower power and cost than flagship GPUs. It is a balanced choice for mixed gaming, creative, and CUDA work.

retrievedChunks.title: RTX 4070 Super gaming profile
retrievedChunks.content: The RTX 4070 Super is efficient for high-quality 1440p gaming and supports CUDA, but its 12GB VRAM is less flexible for larger local AI workloads.

retrievedChunks.title: Fractal North balanced airflow
retrievedChunks.content: The Fractal North supports full-size ATX components with 355mm GPU clearance and 170mm air-cooler clearance. It balances airflow, acoustics, and upgrade room.

retrievedChunks.title: System RAM for local AI
retrievedChunks.content: System RAM does not replace VRAM, but 64GB or more helps with model loading, CPU offload, data preparation, and running supporting services beside a local model.

retrievedChunks.title: 4K 240Hz target trade-off
retrievedChunks.content: A 4K 240Hz target is exceptionally demanding and depends heavily on the game, settings, and upscaling. The recommendation should maximize GPU headroom without claiming that every title will sustain 240 FPS.

retrievedChunks.title: 4K gaming allocation
retrievedChunks.content: At 4K the GPU is normally the primary performance constraint. Budget should favor the graphics card while retaining enough CPU, cooling, and PSU quality to avoid platform imbalance.

retrievedChunks.title: Core i7-14700K high-refresh gaming profile
retrievedChunks.content: The Core i7-14700K is an Intel LGA1700 option for high-refresh gaming and mixed productivity. Its high peak power requires substantial cooling, and the mature socket has less future CPU upgrade room than newer platforms.

retrievedChunks.title: Peerless Assassin value cooling
retrievedChunks.content: The Peerless Assassin 120 SE offers high air-cooling capacity at low cost. It is well suited to mainstream and many high-tier CPUs when case height allows it.

retrievedChunks.title: RTX 5090 flagship profile
retrievedChunks.content: The RTX 5090 combines 32GB GDDR7 with CUDA support and a 575W total graphics power rating. It requires an enthusiast budget, a compatible case, and at least a 1000W system power plan.

result.explanation:
## Summary
This $3,999 build uses an NVIDIA RTX 4090 (24GB VRAM) with 128GB RAM and 2TB storage, targeting 70B Q4 inference and FLUX image generation at 4K. The system is fully compatible except for a power headroom failure, meaning the estimated 971W draw exceeds the PSU capacity.

## Trade-offs
The RTX 4090’s 24GB VRAM is sufficient for the specified AI workloads [K1][K2], but the power headroom failure [FAIL] means the system cannot safely run at full load without upgrading the power supply. The build prioritizes quiet operation and NVIDIA CUDA compliance, but the 4090’s high power draw conflicts with the noise preference under load.

## Upgrade path
Resolve the power headroom failure by replacing the PSU with a higher-wattage unit (e.g., 1200W+) to support sustained AI workloads. No other upgrades are needed for the stated use case, as the RTX 4090 and 128GB RAM already meet the VRAM and capacity requirements [K1][K2].

retrievedChunks.content: The GeForce RTX 5080 reference design combines 16GB GDDR7, CUDA support, a 360W total graphics power rating, and Blackwell media/AI features. It is a high-end 4K and creator option below the RTX 5090, but 16GB VRAM is a meaningful limit for larger local AI models.

retrievedChunks.title: 1440p gaming allocation
retrievedChunks.content: 1440p gaming usually benefits from a GPU-first budget with a balanced modern CPU. It is the broadest value target for high-refresh PC gaming.

retrievedChunks.title: RTX 4090 local AI profile
retrievedChunks.content: The RTX 4090 combines 24GB VRAM with CUDA support. It is a high-cost option best considered when local AI capacity matters more than power draw or compact size.

retrievedChunks.title: RTX 4070 Super gaming profile
retrievedChunks.content: The RTX 4070 Super is efficient for high-quality 1440p gaming and supports CUDA, but its 12GB VRAM is less flexible for larger local AI workloads.

retrievedChunks.title: PCIe 5.0 SSD performance and thermal trade-off
retrievedChunks.content: High-end PCIe 5.0 SSDs such as Samsung 9100 Pro can exceed 14GB/s sequential reads, but cost and heat are materially higher than mature PCIe 4.0 drives. Gaming load times rarely justify allocating budget away from the GPU solely for peak sequential throughput; large creator and data workflows benefit more.

retrievedChunks.title: Ryzen 7 7700 balanced profile
retrievedChunks.content: The Ryzen 7 7700 combines eight cores with a 65W rating. It is a quiet, efficient foundation for development and balanced builds with a useful AM5 upgrade path.

retrievedChunks.title: NVMe capacity for workstation use
retrievedChunks.content: Development and editing systems benefit from 2TB or more of fast NVMe storage for repositories, containers, caches, footage, and active projects.

retrievedChunks.title: 1080p gaming allocation
retrievedChunks.content: At 1080p and high refresh rates, CPU frame delivery matters more than at higher resolutions. GPU budget remains important, but extreme GPU spending can produce diminishing returns.

retrievedChunks.title: Ryzen 9 9950X3D mixed gaming and creation profile
retrievedChunks.content: Ryzen 9 9950X3D combines 16 Zen 5 cores with second-generation 3D V-Cache on AM5. It fits premium systems that need strong gaming latency and high multicore throughput, but it should not displace cheaper eight-core X3D options in gaming-only budgets.

retrievedChunks.title: Core i7-14700K high-refresh gaming profile
retrievedChunks.content: The Core i7-14700K is an Intel LGA1700 option for high-refresh gaming and mixed productivity. Its high peak power requires substantial cooling, and the mature socket has less future CPU upgrade room than newer platforms.

retrievedChunks.title: Peerless Assassin value cooling
retrievedChunks.content: The Peerless Assassin 120 SE offers high air-cooling capacity at low cost. It is well suited to mainstream and many high-tier CPUs when case height allows it.

retrievedChunks.title: Core i7-14700K productivity profile
retrievedChunks.content: The Core i7-14700K offers high multithreaded throughput for editing and compilation, but its peak power and cooling requirements are substantial.

retrievedChunks.title: X670E workstation expansion
retrievedChunks.content: X670E boards add expansion and connectivity for high-tier AM5 workstations. They should be selected for actual I/O or upgrade needs rather than prestige alone.

retrievedChunks.title: B650 platform fit
retrievedChunks.content: A well-equipped B650 motherboard is a sensible mainstream AM5 choice with DDR5 and multiple NVMe slots. Enthusiast CPUs may justify stronger power delivery or an X670E board.

retrievedChunks.title: 850W Gold mainstream high-end fit
retrievedChunks.content: An 850W Gold PSU is a common fit for high-end single-GPU systems when it satisfies the 1.35x power rule. It offers more upgrade margin than 750W without flagship PSU cost.

retrievedChunks.title: Core i7-14700K productivity profile
retrievedChunks.content: The Core i7-14700K offers high multithreaded throughput for editing and compilation, but its peak power and cooling requirements are substantial.

retrievedChunks.title: Memory capacity for development
retrievedChunks.content: Docker, local databases, IDEs, browsers, and emulators compete for memory. 32GB is a practical baseline; 64GB provides meaningful headroom for heavier container and mobile build workflows.

retrievedChunks.title: Fractal North balanced airflow
retrievedChunks.content: The Fractal North supports full-size ATX components with 355mm GPU clearance and 170mm air-cooler clearance. It balances airflow, acoustics, and upgrade room.

retrievedChunks.title: Core i7-14700K high-refresh gaming profile
retrievedChunks.content: The Core i7-14700K is an Intel LGA1700 option for high-refresh gaming and mixed productivity. Its high peak power requires substantial cooling, and the mature socket has less future CPU upgrade room than newer platforms.

retrievedChunks.title: Core Ultra 7 265K LGA1851 profile
retrievedChunks.content: Core Ultra 7 265K has 20 cores and 20 threads, up to 5.5GHz turbo, 125W base power, and 250W maximum turbo power on LGA1851. It is suited to Intel-preference mixed productivity builds but requires a Z890-class motherboard and strong cooling at unrestricted power.

retrievedChunks.title: RTX 4090 local AI profile
retrievedChunks.content: The RTX 4090 combines 24GB VRAM with CUDA support. It is a high-cost option best considered when local AI capacity matters more than power draw or compact size.

retrievedChunks.title: RTX 5060 Ti 16GB capacity-value trade-off
retrievedChunks.content: The 16GB RTX 5060 Ti offers more model and texture capacity than 8GB cards while retaining CUDA support and a 180W reference power target. Its compute throughput is midrange, so capacity-sensitive hobby AI can fit even when raw generation or training speed is below higher-tier GPUs.

retrievedChunks.title: 1080p gaming allocation
retrievedChunks.content: At 1080p and high refresh rates, CPU frame delivery matters more than at higher resolutions. GPU budget remains important, but extreme GPU spending can produce diminishing returns.

retrievedChunks.title: RTX 4070 Super gaming profile
retrievedChunks.content: The RTX 4070 Super is efficient for high-quality 1440p gaming and supports CUDA, but its 12GB VRAM is less flexible for larger local AI workloads.

retrievedChunks.title: PCIe 5.0 SSD performance and thermal trade-off
retrievedChunks.content: High-end PCIe 5.0 SSDs such as Samsung 9100 Pro can exceed 14GB/s sequential reads, but cost and heat are materially higher than mature PCIe 4.0 drives. Gaming load times rarely justify allocating budget away from the GPU solely for peak sequential throughput; large creator and data workflows benefit more.

retrievedChunks.title: X670E workstation expansion
retrievedChunks.content: X670E boards add expansion and connectivity for high-tier AM5 workstations. They should be selected for actual I/O or upgrade needs rather than prestige alone.

retrievedChunks.title: Power supply headroom rule
retrievedChunks.content: A power supply should exceed estimated sustained system draw by at least 35 percent. Additional headroom can reduce fan noise and preserve room for future GPU upgrades.

retrievedChunks.title: NVMe capacity for workstation use
retrievedChunks.content: Development and editing systems benefit from 2TB or more of fast NVMe storage for repositories, containers, caches, footage, and active projects.

retrievedChunks.title: System RAM for local AI
retrievedChunks.content: System RAM does not replace VRAM, but 64GB or more helps with model loading, CPU offload, data preparation, and running supporting services beside a local model.

retrievedChunks.title: AM5 platform upgradeability
retrievedChunks.content: AM5 boards provide a clearer CPU upgrade path than mature end-of-cycle platforms. The value is highest when the user explicitly plans a future CPU upgrade.

retrievedChunks.title: 850W Gold mainstream high-end fit
retrievedChunks.content: An 850W Gold PSU is a common fit for high-end single-GPU systems when it satisfies the 1.35x power rule. It offers more upgrade margin than 750W without flagship PSU cost.

retrievedChunks.title: White dual-chamber showcase case planning
retrievedChunks.content: A white dual-chamber case can provide panoramic presentation, cable separation, and extensive radiator support, but it is physically larger than a conventional mid-tower. Visible-component color matching should remain a preference layer after GPU length, cooler/radiator, motherboard, and PSU compatibility checks.

retrievedChunks.title: RTX 5080 4K and creator profile
retrievedChunks.content: The GeForce RTX 5080 reference design combines 16GB GDDR7, CUDA support, a 360W total graphics power rating, and Blackwell media/AI features. It is a high-end 4K and creator option below the RTX 5090, but 16GB VRAM is a meaningful limit for larger local AI models.

retrievedChunks.title: White dual-chamber showcase case planning
retrievedChunks.content: A white dual-chamber case can provide panoramic presentation, cable separation, and extensive radiator support, but it is physically larger than a conventional mid-tower. Visible-component color matching should remain a preference layer after GPU length, cooler/radiator, motherboard, and PSU compatibility checks.
retrievedChunks.sourceTitle: NZXT H9 Flow RGB official product specifications

retrievedChunks.title: 4K 240Hz target trade-off
retrievedChunks.content: A 4K 240Hz target is exceptionally demanding and depends heavily on the game, settings, and upscaling. The recommendation should maximize GPU headroom without claiming that every title will sustain 240 FPS.

retrievedChunks.title: RTX 5070 Ti balanced high-end profile
retrievedChunks.content: The GeForce RTX 5070 Ti provides 16GB GDDR7 and CUDA at a 300W reference power target. It is positioned for high-refresh 1440p, entry 4K, creator acceleration, and moderate local AI workloads without flagship cost.

retrievedChunks.title: 4K gaming allocation
retrievedChunks.content: At 4K the GPU is normally the primary performance constraint. Budget should favor the graphics card while retaining enough CPU, cooling, and PSU quality to avoid platform imbalance.

retrievedChunks.title: 360mm AIO high-power cooling
retrievedChunks.content: A 360mm AIO provides additional thermal capacity for high-power workstation CPUs, but costs more and requires a compatible radiator mount.

retrievedChunks.title: RTX 4090 local AI profile
retrievedChunks.content: The RTX 4090 combines 24GB VRAM with CUDA support. It is a high-cost option best considered when local AI capacity matters more than power draw or compact size.

retrievedChunks.title: RTX 5090 flagship profile
retrievedChunks.content: The RTX 5090 combines 32GB GDDR7 with CUDA support and a 575W total graphics power rating. It requires an enthusiast budget, a compatible case, and at least a 1000W system power plan.

retrievedChunks.title: Planning a white RGB build
retrievedChunks.content: A coherent white build should prioritize visible components such as the case, graphics card, motherboard heatsinks, memory, and cooler. RGB is an appearance preference and must never override socket, clearance, cooling, or PSU safety rules.

retrievedChunks.title: NVMe capacity for workstation use
retrievedChunks.content: Development and editing systems benefit from 2TB or more of fast NVMe storage for repositories, containers, caches, footage, and active projects.

retrievedChunks.title: White Z790 platform pairing
retrievedChunks.content: A Z790 DDR5 motherboard is the appropriate enthusiast chipset pairing for an unlocked Intel LGA1700 CPU. White or silver heatsink variants can satisfy a light-color build without changing socket or memory compatibility.

retrievedChunks.title: 64GB DDR5 workload fit
retrievedChunks.content: A 64GB DDR5 kit is the default workstation capacity for local AI, development, and video editing when the budget allows.

retrievedChunks.title: PCIe 5.0 SSD performance and thermal trade-off
retrievedChunks.content: High-end PCIe 5.0 SSDs such as Samsung 9100 Pro can exceed 14GB/s sequential reads, but cost and heat are materially higher than mature PCIe 4.0 drives. Gaming load times rarely justify allocating budget away from the GPU solely for peak sequential throughput; large creator and data workflows benefit more.

retrievedChunks.title: Peerless Assassin value cooling
retrievedChunks.content: The Peerless Assassin 120 SE offers high air-cooling capacity at low cost. It is well suited to mainstream and many high-tier CPUs when case height allows it.

retrievedChunks.title: 850W Gold mainstream high-end fit
retrievedChunks.content: An 850W Gold PSU is a common fit for high-end single-GPU systems when it satisfies the 1.35x power rule. It offers more upgrade margin than 750W without flagship PSU cost.

retrievedChunks.title: ATX 3.1 and modern GPU power planning
retrievedChunks.content: For high-draw current GPUs, an ATX 3.1 supply with a native 12V-2x6 cable simplifies cabling and is designed around modern transient requirements. Wattage still must satisfy deterministic sustained-load headroom; the connector standard does not make an undersized PSU acceptable.

retrievedChunks.title: Ryzen 9 7900X workstation profile
retrievedChunks.content: Twelve cores and strong productivity performance make the Ryzen 9 7900X suitable for compilation, rendering, containers, and mixed workstation use. It requires stronger cooling than 65W parts.

retrievedChunks.title: Core i7-14700K productivity profile
retrievedChunks.content: The Core i7-14700K offers high multithreaded throughput for editing and compilation, but its peak power and cooling requirements are substantial.

retrievedChunks.title: 1440p gaming allocation
retrievedChunks.content: 1440p gaming usually benefits from a GPU-first budget with a balanced modern CPU. It is the broadest value target for high-refresh PC gaming.

retrievedChunks.title: RTX 4070 Super gaming profile
retrievedChunks.content: The RTX 4070 Super is efficient for high-quality 1440p gaming and supports CUDA, but its 12GB VRAM is less flexible for larger local AI workloads.

retrievedChunks.title: RTX 4070 Ti Super balanced profile
retrievedChunks.content: The RTX 4070 Ti Super provides 16GB VRAM and strong 1440p to 4K capability at lower power and cost than flagship GPUs. It is a balanced choice for mixed gaming, creative, and CUDA work.

retrievedChunks.title: NR200P small-form-factor constraints
retrievedChunks.content: The NR200P is a Mini-ITX enclosure that requires an SFX power supply and limits tower cooler height. Compact builds require stricter part filtering and usually carry a price premium.

retrievedChunks.title: RTX 5070 Ti balanced high-end profile
retrievedChunks.content: The GeForce RTX 5070 Ti provides 16GB GDDR7 and CUDA at a 300W reference power target. It is positioned for high-refresh 1440p, entry 4K, creator acceleration, and moderate local AI workloads without flagship cost.

retrievedChunks.title: 1080p gaming allocation
retrievedChunks.content: At 1080p and high refresh rates, CPU frame delivery matters more than at higher resolutions. GPU budget remains important, but extreme GPU spending can produce diminishing returns.

retrievedChunks.title: Peerless Assassin value cooling
retrievedChunks.content: The Peerless Assassin 120 SE offers high air-cooling capacity at low cost. It is well suited to mainstream and many high-tier CPUs when case height allows it.

retrievedChunks.title: Fractal North balanced airflow
retrievedChunks.content: The Fractal North supports full-size ATX components with 355mm GPU clearance and 170mm air-cooler clearance. It balances airflow, acoustics, and upgrade room.

retrievedChunks.title: Small-form-factor cost and cooling trade-off
retrievedChunks.content: Mini-ITX systems typically require a Mini-ITX motherboard and SFX power supply and may sharply limit air-cooler height and GPU dimensions. A compact request should trigger hard form-factor filtering and should expect higher component cost or reduced thermal headroom.

retrievedChunks.title: Ryzen 9 9950X3D mixed gaming and creation profile
retrievedChunks.content: Ryzen 9 9950X3D combines 16 Zen 5 cores with second-generation 3D V-Cache on AM5. It fits premium systems that need strong gaming latency and high multicore throughput, but it should not displace cheaper eight-core X3D options in gaming-only budgets.

retrievedChunks.title: Ryzen 9 9950X3D2 developer workstation profile
retrievedChunks.content: Ryzen 9 9950X3D2 combines 16 cores, 32 threads, 208MB total cache, a 200W TDP, and an $899 SEP. It targets high-budget development, compilation, simulation, creation, and gaming systems where both cache capacity and core throughput matter.

retrievedChunks.title: Ryzen 7 9800X3D gaming focus
retrievedChunks.content: The Ryzen 7 9800X3D is an enthusiast gaming CPU. Its value is strongest in high-refresh gaming systems where the GPU is already well funded.

retrievedChunks.title: Case clearance constraints
retrievedChunks.content: GPU length, air-cooler height, motherboard form factor, and PSU form factor are hard physical constraints. These values must be checked deterministically before a case is accepted.

retrievedChunks.title: 4K gaming allocation
retrievedChunks.content: At 4K the GPU is normally the primary performance constraint. Budget should favor the graphics card while retaining enough CPU, cooling, and PSU quality to avoid platform imbalance.

retrievedChunks.title: PCIe 5.0 SSD performance and thermal trade-off
retrievedChunks.content: High-end PCIe 5.0 SSDs such as Samsung 9100 Pro can exceed 14GB/s sequential reads, but cost and heat are materially higher than mature PCIe 4.0 drives. Gaming load times rarely justify allocating budget away from the GPU solely for peak sequential throughput; large creator and data workflows benefit more.

retrievedChunks.title: Cooling capacity rule
retrievedChunks.content: A cooler's thermal rating must meet or exceed the CPU power rating. Quiet builds benefit from additional capacity because the cooler can dissipate heat at lower fan speed.

## Summary
This $2,277 Mini-ITX build targets 1440p 144Hz gaming and coding, using an AMD CPU and Nvidia GPU. All parts are compatible and meet the required SFF, air cooling, SFX PSU, and no-RGB constraints.

## Trade-offs
The RTX 4070 Super’s 12GB VRAM may limit future high-VRAM games or larger AI workloads compared to a 16GB card [K2]. The compact NR200P case restricts cooler height and requires careful part selection, often at a price premium [K4].

## Upgrade Path
A future GPU upgrade to an RTX 4070 Ti Super or RTX 5070 Ti would add 16GB VRAM and improve 1440p longevity [K3][K5]. The SFX PSU and Mini-ITX motherboard limit further internal expansion without a case change.

result.sourceQuery: I need an efficient, low-noise Linux development desktop around $1,300 for VS Code, databases, Docker and occasional 1080p gaming. 64 GB RAM, no RGB, prioritize low power over peak FPS.

retrievedChunks.title: Memory capacity for development
retrievedChunks.content: Docker, local databases, IDEs, browsers, and emulators compete for memory. 32GB is a practical baseline; 64GB provides meaningful headroom for heavier container and mobile build workflows.

retrievedChunks.title: Ryzen 9 9950X3D2 developer workstation profile
retrievedChunks.content: Ryzen 9 9950X3D2 combines 16 cores, 32 threads, 208MB total cache, a 200W TDP, and an $899 SEP. It targets high-budget development, compilation, simulation, creation, and gaming systems where both cache capacity and core throughput matter.

retrievedChunks.title: RTX 5060 Ti 16GB capacity-value trade-off
retrievedChunks.content: The 16GB RTX 5060 Ti offers more model and texture capacity than 8GB cards while retaining CUDA support and a 180W reference power target. Its compute throughput is midrange, so capacity-sensitive hobby AI can fit even when raw generation or training speed is below higher-tier GPUs.

retrievedChunks.title: PCIe 5.0 SSD performance and thermal trade-off
retrievedChunks.content: High-end PCIe 5.0 SSDs such as Samsung 9100 Pro can exceed 14GB/s sequential reads, but cost and heat are materially higher than mature PCIe 4.0 drives. Gaming load times rarely justify allocating budget away from the GPU solely for peak sequential throughput; large creator and data workflows benefit more.

retrievedChunks.title: Ryzen 9 7900X workstation profile
retrievedChunks.content: Twelve cores and strong productivity performance make the Ryzen 9 7900X suitable for compilation, rendering, containers, and mixed workstation use. It requires stronger cooling than 65W parts.

retrievedChunks.title: Core i7-14700K productivity profile
retrievedChunks.content: The Core i7-14700K offers high multithreaded throughput for editing and compilation, but its peak power and cooling requirements are substantial.

retrievedChunks.title: 1080p gaming allocation
retrievedChunks.content: At 1080p and high refresh rates, CPU frame delivery matters more than at higher resolutions. GPU budget remains important, but extreme GPU spending can produce diminishing returns.

retrievedChunks.title: Ryzen 7 7800X3D gaming profile
retrievedChunks.content: The Ryzen 7 7800X3D remains a focused gaming option with lower platform power than many high-core-count alternatives. It is less compelling for heavily threaded workstation tasks.

retrievedChunks.title: Ryzen 7 7700 balanced profile
retrievedChunks.content: The Ryzen 7 7700 combines eight cores with a 65W rating. It is a quiet, efficient foundation for development and balanced builds with a useful AM5 upgrade path.

retrievedChunks.title: Intel Arc B580 value profile
retrievedChunks.content: Intel Arc B580 Limited Edition provides 12GB VRAM, 190W total board power, AV1 media support, and a $249 recommended customer price. It is relevant to value 1080p/1440p builds, while application and game-specific driver behavior should be verified for specialized workloads.
retrievedChunks.sourceTitle: Intel Arc B580 product specifications and ordering

retrievedChunks.title: Radeon RX 9070 XT RDNA 4 profile
retrievedChunks.content: The Radeon RX 9070 XT has 16GB GDDR6, a 304W typical board power rating, and a 750W minimum PSU recommendation from AMD. It is a strong raster and FSR-oriented 4K gaming option, but users requiring CUDA-first software should still prefer NVIDIA.

retrievedChunks.title: RTX 4070 Super gaming profile
retrievedChunks.content: The RTX 4070 Super is efficient for high-quality 1440p gaming and supports CUDA, but its 12GB VRAM is less flexible for larger local AI workloads.

retrievedChunks.title: Fractal North balanced airflow
retrievedChunks.content: The Fractal North supports full-size ATX components with 355mm GPU clearance and 170mm air-cooler clearance. It balances airflow, acoustics, and upgrade room.

retrievedChunks.title: Core Ultra 7 265K LGA1851 profile
retrievedChunks.content: Core Ultra 7 265K has 20 cores and 20 threads, up to 5.5GHz turbo, 125W base power, and 250W maximum turbo power on LGA1851. It is suited to Intel-preference mixed productivity builds but requires a Z890-class motherboard and strong cooling at unrestricted power.

retrievedChunks.title: NVMe capacity for workstation use
retrievedChunks.content: Development and editing systems benefit from 2TB or more of fast NVMe storage for repositories, containers, caches, footage, and active projects.

retrievedChunks.title: Peerless Assassin value cooling
retrievedChunks.content: The Peerless Assassin 120 SE offers high air-cooling capacity at low cost. It is well suited to mainstream and many high-tier CPUs when case height allows it.

retrievedChunks.title: Case clearance constraints
retrievedChunks.content: GPU length, air-cooler height, motherboard form factor, and PSU form factor are hard physical constraints. These values must be checked deterministically before a case is accepted.

retrievedChunks.title: Cooling capacity rule
retrievedChunks.content: A cooler's thermal rating must meet or exceed the CPU power rating. Quiet builds benefit from additional capacity because the cooler can dissipate heat at lower fan speed.

## Summary
This $1,258 build delivers a 16-core Ryzen 9 9950X3D2 CPU with 64GB RAM and an RTX 5060 Ti 16GB GPU, optimized for Linux development (VS Code, databases, Docker) and occasional 1080p gaming. The system prioritizes low power and quiet operation within budget, with all components compatible.

## Trade-offs
The high-core CPU and 64GB RAM provide exceptional headroom for containers and databases [K1][K2], but the GPU is midrange, limiting gaming FPS at 1080p. The PCIe 5.0 SSD adds cost and heat for minimal gaming benefit [K4], while the 200W CPU TDP conflicts slightly with the low-power preference [K2].

## Upgrade path
Adding a higher-tier GPU (e.g., RTX 5070) would improve gaming performance without changing the platform. The 64GB RAM and 16-core CPU already exceed current development needs, so no near-term CPU or memory upgrade is necessary.

retrievedChunks.title: 1440p gaming allocation
retrievedChunks.content: 1440p gaming usually benefits from a GPU-first budget with a balanced modern CPU. It is the broadest value target for high-refresh PC gaming.

retrievedChunks.title: RX 7800 XT 1440p value
retrievedChunks.content: The RX 7800 XT is a strong value target for 1440p gaming with 16GB VRAM. It favors raster performance per dollar over CUDA ecosystem support.

retrievedChunks.title: 1080p gaming allocation
retrievedChunks.content: At 1080p and high refresh rates, CPU frame delivery matters more than at higher resolutions. GPU budget remains important, but extreme GPU spending can produce diminishing returns.

retrievedChunks.title: Radeon RX 9070 XT RDNA 4 profile
retrievedChunks.content: The Radeon RX 9070 XT has 16GB GDDR6, a 304W typical board power rating, and a 750W minimum PSU recommendation from AMD. It is a strong raster and FSR-oriented 4K gaming option, but users requiring CUDA-first software should still prefer NVIDIA.

retrievedChunks.title: RX 7900 XTX raster value
retrievedChunks.content: The RX 7900 XTX offers 24GB VRAM and excellent high-resolution raster performance. It is compelling for 4K gaming when CUDA compatibility is not required.

retrievedChunks.title: Ryzen 9 9950X3D mixed gaming and creation profile
retrievedChunks.content: Ryzen 9 9950X3D combines 16 Zen 5 cores with second-generation 3D V-Cache on AM5. It fits premium systems that need strong gaming latency and high multicore throughput, but it should not displace cheaper eight-core X3D options in gaming-only budgets.

retrievedChunks.title: White Z790 platform pairing
retrievedChunks.content: A Z790 DDR5 motherboard is the appropriate enthusiast chipset pairing for an unlocked Intel LGA1700 CPU. White or silver heatsink variants can satisfy a light-color build without changing socket or memory compatibility.

retrievedChunks.title: Ryzen 7 7800X3D gaming profile
retrievedChunks.content: The Ryzen 7 7800X3D remains a focused gaming option with lower platform power than many high-core-count alternatives. It is less compelling for heavily threaded workstation tasks.

retrievedChunks.title: Ryzen 7 9800X3D gaming focus
retrievedChunks.content: The Ryzen 7 9800X3D is an enthusiast gaming CPU. Its value is strongest in high-refresh gaming systems where the GPU is already well funded.

retrievedChunks.title: Ryzen 9 9950X3D2 developer workstation profile
retrievedChunks.content: Ryzen 9 9950X3D2 combines 16 cores, 32 threads, 208MB total cache, a 200W TDP, and an $899 SEP. It targets high-budget development, compilation, simulation, creation, and gaming systems where both cache capacity and core throughput matter.

retrievedChunks.title: Peerless Assassin value cooling
retrievedChunks.content: The Peerless Assassin 120 SE offers high air-cooling capacity at low cost. It is well suited to mainstream and many high-tier CPUs when case height allows it.

retrievedChunks.title: Fractal North balanced airflow
retrievedChunks.content: The Fractal North supports full-size ATX components with 355mm GPU clearance and 170mm air-cooler clearance. It balances airflow, acoustics, and upgrade room.

retrievedChunks.title: Planning a white RGB build
retrievedChunks.content: A coherent white build should prioritize visible components such as the case, graphics card, motherboard heatsinks, memory, and cooler. RGB is an appearance preference and must never override socket, clearance, cooling, or PSU safety rules.

retrievedChunks.title: White dual-chamber showcase case planning
retrievedChunks.content: A white dual-chamber case can provide panoramic presentation, cable separation, and extensive radiator support, but it is physically larger than a conventional mid-tower. Visible-component color matching should remain a preference layer after GPU length, cooler/radiator, motherboard, and PSU compatibility checks.
retrievedChunks.sourceTitle: NZXT H9 Flow RGB official product specifications

retrievedChunks.title: 4K gaming allocation
retrievedChunks.content: At 4K the GPU is normally the primary performance constraint. Budget should favor the graphics card while retaining enough CPU, cooling, and PSU quality to avoid platform imbalance.

retrievedChunks.title: PCIe 5.0 SSD performance and thermal trade-off
retrievedChunks.content: High-end PCIe 5.0 SSDs such as Samsung 9100 Pro can exceed 14GB/s sequential reads, but cost and heat are materially higher than mature PCIe 4.0 drives. Gaming load times rarely justify allocating budget away from the GPU solely for peak sequential throughput; large creator and data workflows benefit more.

retrievedChunks.title: Case clearance constraints
retrievedChunks.content: GPU length, air-cooler height, motherboard form factor, and PSU form factor are hard physical constraints. These values must be checked deterministically before a case is accepted.

retrievedChunks.title: 850W Gold mainstream high-end fit
retrievedChunks.content: An 850W Gold PSU is a common fit for high-end single-GPU systems when it satisfies the 1.35x power rule. It offers more upgrade margin than 750W without flagship PSU cost.

result.explanation:
## Summary
This AMD-only build targets 1440p 144fps gaming at ¥13,143, well under the ¥15,000 budget. All components are compatible, and the RX 7800 XT provides strong raster performance with 16GB VRAM for your resolution and frame rate goals.

## Trade-offs
The RX 7800 XT may struggle to consistently hit 144fps in demanding titles at 1440p, especially with ray tracing enabled [K2]. The white/RGB aesthetic and quiet operation preferences slightly increase cost versus equivalent non-themed parts.

## Upgrade path
A future GPU upgrade to the RX 9070 XT or RX 7900 XTX would improve 1440p frame rates and add headroom for 4K gaming [K4][K5]. The 750W PSU supports these upgrades, but verify physical clearance in your case.

retrievedChunks.title: RTX 5080 4K and creator profile
retrievedChunks.content: The GeForce RTX 5080 reference design combines 16GB GDDR7, CUDA support, a 360W total graphics power rating, and Blackwell media/AI features. It is a high-end 4K and creator option below the RTX 5090, but 16GB VRAM is a meaningful limit for larger local AI models.

retrievedChunks.title: Ryzen 9 9950X3D2 developer workstation profile
retrievedChunks.content: Ryzen 9 9950X3D2 combines 16 cores, 32 threads, 208MB total cache, a 200W TDP, and an $899 SEP. It targets high-budget development, compilation, simulation, creation, and gaming systems where both cache capacity and core throughput matter.

retrievedChunks.title: Ryzen 9 7900X workstation profile
retrievedChunks.content: Twelve cores and strong productivity performance make the Ryzen 9 7900X suitable for compilation, rendering, containers, and mixed workstation use. It requires stronger cooling than 65W parts.

retrievedChunks.title: 4K gaming allocation
retrievedChunks.content: At 4K the GPU is normally the primary performance constraint. Budget should favor the graphics card while retaining enough CPU, cooling, and PSU quality to avoid platform imbalance.

retrievedChunks.title: RTX 5070 Ti balanced high-end profile
retrievedChunks.content: The GeForce RTX 5070 Ti provides 16GB GDDR7 and CUDA at a 300W reference power target. It is positioned for high-refresh 1440p, entry 4K, creator acceleration, and moderate local AI workloads without flagship cost.

retrievedChunks.title: Ryzen 7 7700 balanced profile
retrievedChunks.content: The Ryzen 7 7700 combines eight cores with a 65W rating. It is a quiet, efficient foundation for development and balanced builds with a useful AM5 upgrade path.

retrievedChunks.title: 4K 240Hz target trade-off
retrievedChunks.content: A 4K 240Hz target is exceptionally demanding and depends heavily on the game, settings, and upscaling. The recommendation should maximize GPU headroom without claiming that every title will sustain 240 FPS.

retrievedChunks.title: RTX 4070 Ti Super balanced profile
retrievedChunks.content: The RTX 4070 Ti Super provides 16GB VRAM and strong 1440p to 4K capability at lower power and cost than flagship GPUs. It is a balanced choice for mixed gaming, creative, and CUDA work.

retrievedChunks.title: RTX 4070 Super gaming profile
retrievedChunks.content: The RTX 4070 Super is efficient for high-quality 1440p gaming and supports CUDA, but its 12GB VRAM is less flexible for larger local AI workloads.

retrievedChunks.title: PCIe 5.0 SSD performance and thermal trade-off
retrievedChunks.content: High-end PCIe 5.0 SSDs such as Samsung 9100 Pro can exceed 14GB/s sequential reads, but cost and heat are materially higher than mature PCIe 4.0 drives. Gaming load times rarely justify allocating budget away from the GPU solely for peak sequential throughput; large creator and data workflows benefit more.

retrievedChunks.title: 64GB DDR5 workload fit

retrievedChunks.title: NVMe capacity for workstation use
retrievedChunks.content: Development and editing systems benefit from 2TB or more of fast NVMe storage for repositories, containers, caches, footage, and active projects.

retrievedChunks.title: 360mm AIO high-power cooling
retrievedChunks.content: A 360mm AIO provides additional thermal capacity for high-power workstation CPUs, but costs more and requires a compatible radiator mount.

## Summary
The build pairs a Ryzen 9 9950X3D with an RTX 5090, 128GB RAM, and 4TB storage for $6,753, targeting Unreal Engine 5, Blender Cycles, and large C++ builds at 4K 60fps. All parts are compatible, but the total exceeds the $6,500 budget by $253.

## Trade-offs
The power headroom warning indicates the PSU may be insufficient for peak loads, risking stability during sustained compilation or rendering. The RTX 5090’s 32GB VRAM is ideal for development, but the 898W estimated draw requires a higher-wattage PSU to ensure reliability [K5].

## Upgrade path
No upgrade path is provided because the user did not prioritize upgradeability. The existing CPU and GPU are already top-tier, so future improvements would require a higher-capacity PSU or faster storage.
